personas.name: Frontend Developer
personas.role: Using haematite in a browser application

stories.text: As a frontend developer, I want to use the same haematite API in the browser as on the server so that I do not need to learn a separate client-side storage library.
As a frontend developer, I want the WASM binary to be under 2MB gzipped so that my application's initial load time is not dominated by the database engine.
As a frontend developer, I want haematite to run in a web worker so that database operations do not block the UI thread.
As a frontend developer, I want to fork and merge databases in the browser so that I can implement offline editing with later sync.
As an application developer, I want the browser haematite instance to sync with the server by exchanging content-addressed nodes so that offline changes merge cleanly when connectivity returns.
As an application developer, I want content-addressed hashes to be identical between the server and browser so that sync works by hash comparison without translation.
As an application developer, I want the WAL to use OPFS when available and fall back to IndexedDB when it is not so that durability is best-effort without configuration.

personas.name: Edge Developer
personas.role: Running haematite in edge compute environments

stories.text: As an edge developer, I want haematite to run in Cloudflare Workers or Deno Deploy so that I can embed a branchable database at the edge.
As an edge developer, I want the WASM build to have no native-only dependencies so that it runs in any WASM-capable runtime.
As an operations engineer, I want to verify that the WASM build produces identical hashes to the native build so that sync between native and WASM nodes is reliable.
As an operations engineer, I want the WASM build to gracefully degrade when browser APIs (OPFS, web workers) are unavailable so that the database is functional in constrained environments.
